elements.sent-2411-001: Growing planets interact with their surrounding protoplanetary disk, generating feedback effects that may promote or suppress nearby planet formation.
elements.sent-2411-002: We study how spiral waves launched by planets affect the motion and collisional evolution of particles in the disk.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we perform local 2D hydrodynamical simulations that include a gap-opening planet and integrate particle trajectories within the gas field.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our results show that particle trajectories bend at the location of the spiral wave, and collisions occurring within the spiral exhibit significantly enhanced collisional velocities compared to elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-005: To quantify this effect, we ran simulations with varying planetary masses and particle sizes.
elements.sent-2411-006: The resulting collisional velocities within the spiral far exceed the typical fragmentation threshold, even for collisions between particles of relatively similar sizes and for planetary masses below the pebble isolation mass.
elements.sent-2411-007: If collisions within the spiral are frequent, this effect could lead to progressively smaller particle sizes as the radial distance from the planet decreases, impacting processes such as gap filtering, pebble accretion, and planetesimal formation.
elements.sent-2411-008: Growing planets launch spiral waves in the protoplanetary disk, the prominence of which depends predominantly on the mass of the planet, the thermodynamics, and the viscosity of the gas \citep{Paardekooper2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the case of massive planets, the torque exerted on the disk drives gas away from the planetary orbit, resulting in the opening of a planetary gap.
elements.sent-2411-010: The pressure maximum that forms at the gap edge can act to trap dust and is often invoked to explain the presence of gaps and rings in observations of protoplanetary disks (e.g. \citealt{Dipierro2015, Zhang2018 ).}
elements.sent-2411-011: As the disk evolves, initially micron-sized dust grains collide and grow to form larger particles called pebbles.
elements.sent-2411-012: Further collisional growth is impaired by radial drift, fragmentation and bouncing \citep{Birnstiel2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Radial drift occurs because gas in the disk orbits at a sub-Keplerian velocity, causing pebbles that move at closer to Keplerian velocity to experience a headwind, lose angular momentum, and drift towards the star \citep{Adachi1976,Weidenschilling1977 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Particles are also subject to turbulent mixing \citep{Youdin2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The resulting velocities from all of these effects depend on how well coupled the particles are to the gas.
elements.sent-2411-016: The relative velocity between pairs of particles increases as the difference in particle size becomes larger.
elements.sent-2411-017: High-velocity collisions tend to result in fragmentation rather than sticking, with collision experiments finding fragmentation velocities, $v_{\rm frag}$, of about $1-10\, m\, s^{-1}$ \citep{BlumWurm2008,GundlachBlum2015,MusiolikWurm2019,Musiolik2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this work, we study whether fragmentation can also be enhanced inside the planet-induced spiral wave.
elements.sent-2411-019: To this end, we perform local 2D hydrodynamical simulations of planet-disk interactions and integrate the orbits of particles on top of the gas field.
elements.sent-2411-020: We adopt the local-shearing-sheet approximation \citep{GoldreichLyndenBell1965 to model the evolution of a razor-thin protoplanetary disk perturbed by a planet.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Our methodology closely follows that of \citet{YangZhu2020 for the limit of no dust back-reaction, with the exception of added viscosity to allow for gas-gap equilibrium.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Below is a summary of our model; for a detailed description, we refer to \citet{YangZhu2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The protoplanetary disk considered in this work is isothermal, non-self-gravitating, non-magnetized and razor-thin.
elements.sent-2411-024: We model a local patch of the disk located at an arbitrary radial distance $r=r_0$ from the star, which rotates around the star with the Keplerian angular frequency $\Omega_{\rm K}$.
elements.sent-2411-025: The shearing sheet is mapped by a fixed regular grid with coordinates $x=r-r_0$ and $y=r(\theta-\theta_0)$, where $\theta_0$ is an arbitrary reference azimuthal angle.
elements.sent-2411-026: All velocities are calculated with respect to the background Keplerian shear velocity $-3\Omega_{\rm K x\hat{\bf{e _y/2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The gaseous component is evolved with the PENCIL CODE \citep{BrandenburgDobler2002,Pencil2021 , which employs sixth-order spatial derivatives and a third-order Runge-Kutta method for time integration.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We impose a constant radial pressure gradient such that $\Delta u / c_{\rm s = 0.05$, where $\Delta u$ is the reduction in azimuthal gas velocity compared to the Keplerian velocity, and $c_{\rm s}$ is the sound speed.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The formulation and implementation of the viscous stress tensor is described by Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-030: We approximate the viscosity using the dimensionless alpha approach, $\nu = \alpha \Omega_{\rm K H_{\rm g ^2$ \citep{ShakuraSunyaev1973 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Here, $H_{\rm g =c_{\rm s /\Omega_{\rm K}$ is the vertical gas scale height, and $\alpha$ governs the efficiency of turbulent transport.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We use a constant value of $\alpha=0.01$ throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-033: The planet is placed at the centre of the shearing sheet and modelled by a smoothed gravitational potential, \begin{equation}\Phi(d) = -GM_{\rm p} \frac{d^2+3r_{\rm s}^2/2}{(d^2+r_{\rm s}^2)^{3/2}},\end{equation} where $G$ is the gravitational constant, $M_{\rm p}$ is the planetary mass, $d=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$ is the distance to the planet, and $r_{\rm s}$ is the smoothing length \citep{Dong2011,Zhu2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The planetary mass is defined in terms of the thermal mass, \begin{equation}M_{\rm th}=\frac{c_{\rm s}^3}{G\Omega_{\rm K}},\end{equation} and we adopt a smoothing length of $r_{\rm s =0.8R_{\rm H}$, where \begin{equation}R_{\rm H}=r_0\left(\frac{M_{\rm p}}{3M_*}\right)^{1/3}=H_{\rm g}\left(\frac{M_{\rm p}}{3M_{\rm th}}\right)^{1/3}\end{equation} is the Hill radius.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The gas is initialized in equilibrium state with a constant surface density $\Sigma_0$; in other words, the global radial pressure gradient is imposed as an external background.
elements.sent-2411-036: The gravitational force from the planet is gradually increased from 0 to its full magnitude over $10P$ by varying $G$, where $P=2\pi/\Omega_{\rm K}$ is the orbital period.
elements.sent-2411-037: We adopt a large computational domain of $16H_{\rm g}$ in the radial dimension and $32H_{\rm g}$ in the azimuthal dimension, with a fixed resolution of 32 cells per $H_{\rm g}$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Standard sheared-periodic boundary conditions are employed \citep{Brandenburg1995, Hawley1995 , along with a damping zone of width $1H_{\rm g}$ near the radial boundary to damp waves generated by the planet (Eq. 4-6 in \citealt{YangZhu2020 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-039: We perform simulations with $M_{\rm p /M_{\rm th = 0.25$, $0.5$, $0.75$ and $1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Each system is evolved up to $t=200P$, by which point all systems have long reached equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our gap depths agree within 10\% of the fit provided by \citet{Kanagawa2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: We use the equilibrium state of the gas disk at $t=200P$ to post-process the simulations and track the movement of particles.
elements.sent-2411-043: The particles are modelled as Lagrangian particles, with Stokes number $\St=\Omega_{\rm K t_{\rm s}$, where $t_{\rm s}$ is the characteristic time-scale for reducing the relative velocity between a particle and its surrounding gas by the drag force.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The Stokes number predominantly depends on particle size and the local gas density, with the peak drift velocity occurring for particles with $\St=1$.
elements.sent-2411-045: In the PENCIL CODE simulations of \citet{YangZhu2020 , the Stokes number was kept constant as the particles moved through the gas disk.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In our post-processing routine, we take the dependence of gas density into account by using: $t_{\rm s = t_{\rm s,0 / (\Sigma_{\rm g /\Sigma_0)$ \citep{Weidenschilling1977 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The equations of motion for each particle are: \begin{equation}\frac{d\bf{x}_{\rm p}}{dt} = {\bf v_{\rm p}}-\frac{3}{2}\Omega_{\rm K}x_{\rm p}\hat{\bf{y}},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\frac{d\bf{v}_{\rm p}}{dt} = \left(2\Omega_{\rm K}v_{p,y}\hat{\bf{x}} - \frac{1}{2}\Omega_{\rm K}v_{p,x}\hat{\bf{y}} \right) + \frac{\bf{u}-\bf{v_p}}{t_{\rm s}} - \nabla\Phi,\end{equation} where $\bf{x _{\rm p}$ is the particle position, and $\bf{v _{\rm p}$ is the particle velocity relative to the background shear flow.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We integrate the equations of motion using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta method and apply bilinear interpolation to determine the gas velocity $\bf{u}$ and $\Sigma_{\rm g}$ at the particle positions.
elements.sent-2411-049: The particles are introduced just interior of the radial damping zone, with random azimuthal positions and initial drift velocities as described by \citet{Adachi1976 and \citet{Weidenschilling1977 .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We consider particles with $\St=0.1$, $0.075$, $0.05$, $0.025$, $0.01$, and as discussed in Section~\ref{sect: results , we only integrate one trajectory for each St.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The particle trajectories are integrated until one of the following occurs: (a) the particle passes through the gap; (b) the particle enters a region within $0.2R_{\rm H}$ of the planet; or (c) the particle's drift halts at the gap edge.
elements.sent-2411-052: Since the background gas field is constant and there is no random particle motion, the particle trajectories are deterministic.
elements.sent-2411-053: We compared post-processed results using constant Stokes number against particle trajectories evolved with the Pencil Code and found excellent agreement.
elements.sent-2411-054: We also confirmed that the radial drift velocity far from the planet agrees with the analytical drift rate assuming a zero radial gas velocity.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.63\columnwidth]{Figures_new/yVSx_rho_05Mth.png} \caption{Left: Gas surface density at $t=200\, P$ from our simulation with $M_{\rm p}/M_{\rm th} = 0.5$, with gas streamlines overlaid. The azimuthally averaged gas surface density profile is shown in the top right corner. Right: Same as the left panel, but zoomed in on a small radial portion of the domain. Trajectories for particles with $\St=0.1$ and $\St=0.05$ are shown with black and red lines, respectively. Scatter points mark the locations where the trajectories intersect. Gas streamlines and particle trajectories bend at the location of the spiral wave. } \label{fig:yVSx_rho}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{Figures_new/yVSx_rho_Vcoll.png} \caption{The scatter points show the locations and collisional velocities at all intersection points between the trajectories of particles with $\St=0.05$ and $\St=0.01$, for all considered planetary masses. The gas surface density is shown in the background, using the same color scale as in Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_rho}. Collisional velocities are significantly enhanced for collisions occurring inside the spiral wave. The collisional velocity increases with planetary mass, decreases with radial distance from the planet, and decreases with distance from the center of the spiral wave. } \label{fig:yVSx_rho_Vcol}
elements.sent-2411-055: Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_rho shows the gas surface density, gas streamlines, and trajectories of particles with $\St=0.1$ and $\St=0.05$ from our simulation with $M_{\rm p /M_{\rm th = 0.5$ (see Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_ux in the Appendix for color maps of the gas velocity field).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The planet induces a pair of spiral arms that wrap around the azimuthal periodic boundary and propagate radially away from the planet until they reach the radial damping zone, where they are damped.
elements.sent-2411-057: The planet exerts a torque on the gas, resulting in gap formation.
elements.sent-2411-058: Simultaneously, the viscous force acts to refill the gap, eventually leading to an equilibrium state.
elements.sent-2411-059: Gas located deep within the gap executes horseshoe orbits relative to the planet.
elements.sent-2411-060: Further away, the streamlines are relatively straight but bend at the location of the spiral wave.
elements.sent-2411-061: This bending of the streamlines decreases radially away from the planet, as the spiral arms become less prominent.
elements.sent-2411-062: The right panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_rho shows how both the gas streamlines and particle trajectories bend at the location of the spiral wave.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The particle trajectories bend less than the gas streamlines, and particles with larger $\St$ bend less than those with smaller $\St$, which are more closely coupled to the gas.
elements.sent-2411-064: Given that our particle trajectories are deterministic, and since we do not aim to constrain collisional probabilities in this work, we limit our study to one integrated particle trajectory per combination of $\St$ and $M_{\rm p}$.
elements.sent-2411-065: To study particle collisions, we find all intersections between trajectories of particles with different $\St$ and use linear interpolation in $x$ and $y$ to obtain the velocities at the intersection points.
elements.sent-2411-066: This approach ignores the time dimension; rather, it assumes a constant stream of particles along the trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-067: In the right panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_rho , we highlight intersections between trajectories of particles with $\St=0.1$ and $\St=0.05$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The relative velocity at the intersection point, henceforth referred to as the collisional velocity, $v_{\rm coll}$, is obtained as: \begin{equation}v_{\rm coll} = \sqrt{\Delta v_{p,x}^2 + \Delta v_{p,y}^2},\end{equation} where $\Delta v_{p,x/y}$ is the x/y-component of particle velocity, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-069: If the calculated collisional velocity exceeds the fragmentation velocity, it implies that if the two particles were to collide at that location, they would likely fragment (see Section~\ref{sect:conclusions for a discussion on mass transfer).}
elements.sent-2411-070: Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_rho_Vcol shows the locations of all intersections between our trajectories of particles with $\St=0.05$ and $\St=0.01$, along with the corresponding collisional velocities, for all four planetary masses considered.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Collisions occurring inside the spiral wave exhibit significantly higher collisional velocities than those between the spirals.
elements.sent-2411-072: The collisional velocity within the spiral decreases both radially away from the planet and with distance from the center of the spiral.
elements.sent-2411-073: Collisional velocities increase with planetary mass, and for the highest planetary mass considered, we obtain velocities exceeding 20\% of the sound speed near the gap edge.
elements.sent-2411-074: Efficient fragmentation at the gap edge was also reported by \citet{Drazkowska2019 , who used a 2D coagulation model to study dust evolution in the presence of a Jupiter-mass planet.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{Figures_new/velocityVSr.png} \caption{The colored areas indicate the region between the 10th and 90th percentiles of the same collision velocities shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_rho_Vcol}, converted to SI units by assuming the sound speed profile by \citet{ChiangGoldreich1997}, but limited to collisions occurring within $y=\pm 0.5\, H_{\rm g}$ of the spiral center. Overlaid are the typically assumed fragmentation velocities and the differential drift velocity as a function of semimajor axis.} \label{fig:velocityVSr}
elements.sent-2411-075: In Fig.~\ref{fig:velocityVSr , we compare the collisional velocities within the spiral wave to other relevant velocities, again for collisions occurring between particles of $\St=0.05$ and $\St=0.01$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The shown differential drift velocity, $v_{\rm rel,drift}$, is the difference in radial drift velocity for particles of $\St=0.05$ and $\St=0.01$ \citep{Weidenschilling1977 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: We convert to SI units by multiplying by the sound speed, which is calculated assuming a temperature profile of $T = 150\, K\times (r/au)^{-3/7}$ \citep{ChiangGoldreich1997 and a mean molecular weight of 2.34 \citep{Hayashi1981 .}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The majority of collisions occurring within the spiral wave happen at velocities well above the typically assumed fragmentation threshold.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{Figures_new/VcollVSrho.png} \caption{Collisional velocity as a function of local gas surface density for collisions between all particle pairs with different $\St$ considered in this work. The colors represent the difference in $\St$ between the colliding particles. The collisional velocity increases with $\Delta \St$, local gas surface density, and planetary mass, up to the gap-opening mass.} \label{fig:VcollVSrho}
elements.sent-2411-079: Fig.~\ref{fig:VcollVSrho shows the collisional velocity as a function of the local gas density at the intersection points, with the color of the scatter points indicating the difference in $\St$ between the colliding particles.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The gas density enhancement inside the spiral decreases radially away from the planet and with distance away from the spiral center, making it a useful tracer of the spiral's strength.
elements.sent-2411-081: The collisional velocity increases with both local gas density and planetary mass, following the same trend observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_rho_Vcol .}
elements.sent-2411-082: As expected, the collisional velocity also increases with $\Delta \St$, since particles with very different $\St$ follow very different trajectories as they cross the spiral wave, resulting in larger relative velocities.
elements.sent-2411-083: Collisions between particles near the gap edge can result in collisional velocities exceeding 35\% of the sound speed when large planetary masses are considered.
elements.sent-2411-084: Even for $\Delta \St = 0.025$, collisional velocities exceeding 5-10\% of the sound speed are commonly obtained, which can still be above the fragmentation limit in most regions of the disk (see Fig.~\ref{fig:velocityVSr ).}
elements.sent-2411-085: The same analysis performed for simulations using constant $\St$ resulted in only up to $\sim$ 20\% higher collision velocities, implying that the gas velocity field and the bending of particle trajectories are the dominant cause for the enhanced collision velocities within the spiral, with non-axisymmetric density variations secondary.
elements.sent-2411-086: We have studied the motion of particles in the presence of embedded and gap-opening planets.
elements.sent-2411-087: Our results show that particle trajectories bend at the location of the planet-induced spiral wave.
elements.sent-2411-088: We integrated the trajectories of particles with different $\St$ and identified all intersections between particles with different $\St$, without accounting for the timing of the collisions.
elements.sent-2411-089: The collisional velocity at these intersection points was calculated, revealing significantly higher collisional velocities within the spiral compared to elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-090: The collisional velocity within the spiral increases with planetary mass and $\Delta \St$ of the colliding particles, and decreases with both radial distance from the planet and distance from the center of the spiral wave.
elements.sent-2411-091: The velocities obtained predominantly exceed the fragmentation velocity, suggesting that collisions occurring within the spiral likely would result in fragmentation.
elements.sent-2411-092: In our discussion so far, we have assumed that collisions occurring at velocities above the fragmentation threshold result in mass loss.
elements.sent-2411-093: This is the common assumption utilized in most studies and simulations of dust growth; however, laboratory experiments find that such collisions can result in a mass gain of the larger particle if the smaller particle is small enough (see \citealt{Johansen2014 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-094: The efficiency of this mass transfer decreases with increasing collision velocity \citep{Meisner2013 , suggesting that the overall efficiency could be quite low for many of the high-velocity collisions considered in this study.}
elements.sent-2411-095: It is also unclear what size difference that is required to be in the mass transfer regime, and the pairs of collisions considered in this work may not be over this threshold size difference, especially the small $\Delta \St$ pairs.
elements.sent-2411-096: Coagulation typically results in peaked size distributions where most of the mass is concentrated in the largest particles, meaning that most of the mass should be in particles with similar $\St$ \citep{Birnstiel2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: We find collisional velocities exceeding 5-10\% of the sound speed, corresponding to velocities above the typical fragmentation limit, even for $\Delta \St = 0.025$.
elements.sent-2411-098: For comparison, the relative velocity expected from collisions between similarly sized particles in turbulence is: \begin{equation}\label{eq: vrel} v_{\rm rel, turb} = \sqrt{3\alpha_{\rm turb} \St}c_{\rm s}\end{equation} \citep{OrmelCuzzi2007 .}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq: vrel} v_{\rm rel, turb} = \sqrt{3\alpha_{\rm turb} \St}c_{\rm s}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-099: Observations of protoplanetary disks generally suggest turbulent parameters on the order of $\alpha_{\rm turb \sim 10^{-5 -10^{-3}$ \citep{Pinte2023 , which corresponds to collisional velocities of at most a few percent of the sound speed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The impact of our results on dust evolution depends on the frequency of collisions within the spiral wave and whether the timescale for re-coagulation is faster or slower than the time between spiral wave passages (i.e., the orbital period).
elements.sent-2411-101: The dust growth timescale can be estimated as: \begin{equation}\tau_{\rm grow} \simeq \frac{1}{\Omega_{\rm K}\Sigma_{\rm dust}/\Sigma_{\rm g}},\end{equation} for similarly sized particles and relative velocities given by Eq.~\ref{eq: vrel \citep{Birnstiel2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-102: For a typical dust-to-gas ratio of 1\%, this corresponds to a growth timescale that is much longer than the orbital period.
elements.sent-2411-103: The model we present does not provide information on the frequency of collisions within the spiral wave.
elements.sent-2411-104: If collisions are common, our results suggest an increased amount of fragmentation, leading to progressively smaller particle sizes as the radial distance from the planet decreases.
elements.sent-2411-105: Smaller particles can cross the planetary gap more easily, as shown by \citet{Drazkowska2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Our findings thus support the idea that planetary gaps are leaky \citep{Stammler2023 , and may challenge the idea that the isotope dichotomy between carbonaceous (CC) and non-carbonaceous (NC) meteorites arose due to Jupiter's core forming early and separating the inner and outer Solar System into two separate reservoirs \citep{Kruijer2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Additionally, smaller particles have a lower drift velocity, which could result in a buildup of particles beyond the planet's orbit, starting already beyond the gap edge.
elements.sent-2411-108: Furthermore, small particles are less efficiently accreted onto planets via pebble accretion \citep{Johansen2017 and are less efficient in forming planetesimals via the streaming instability \citep{Yang2017,LiYoudin2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The study that is presented is idealized and does not account for effects such as turbulence, dust-back reaction, dust coagulation or collisional probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-110: Our aim is to demonstrate the potential importance of particle fragmentation within planet-induced spiral waves.
elements.sent-2411-111: Additionally, our study is performed in 2D, neglecting any 3D effects.
elements.sent-2411-112: Spiral waves are in general weaker in 3D than in 2D, and the density contrast can be reduced by about a half \citep{Tanaka2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-113: On the other hand, it remains unclear if the velocity perturbation in 3D is reduced by as much, and it can remain high in terms of the speed of sound \citep{Zhu2015,RabagoZhu2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-114: As we have determined that the major driver of particle collisions is the velocity field in the gas instead of the density field, the collision speeds may remain high and over fragmentation threshold in 3D disks.
elements.sent-2411-115: To address the importance of our results for dust evolution and planet formation, future studies addressing all of the above considerations are necessary.

elements.sent-2411-123: Fig.~\ref{fig:yVSx_ux shows the x- and y-component of the gas velocity field at the equilibrium state from our simulation with $M_{\rm p /M_{\rm th = 0.5$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The velocity perturbations driven by the planet across spiral arms can be as high as $\sim$ 50\% the speed of sound.
elements.sent-2411-125: These perturbations are the likely source to drive high collision speeds between particles of different sizes within the spiral arms.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1.67\columnwidth]{Figures_new/yVSx_u_05Mth.png} \caption{The x- and y-component of the gas velocity field at the equilibrium state from our simulation with $M_{\rm p}/M_{\rm th} = 0.5$. The background Keplerian shear has been removed from the velocity field.} \label{fig:yVSx_ux}
elements.sent-2411-126: This is a guide for preparing papers for Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society using the \verb'mnras' \LaTeX\ package.
elements.sent-2411-127: It provides instructions for using the additional features in the document class.
elements.sent-2411-128: This is not a general guide on how to use \LaTeX, and nor does it replace the journal's instructions to authors.
elements.sent-2411-129: See mnras\_template.tex for a simple template.
elements.sent-2411-130: The journal Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society (MNRAS) encourages authors to prepare their papers using \LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-131: The style file \verb'mnras.cls' can be used to approximate the final appearance of the journal, and provides numerous features to simplify the preparation of papers.
elements.sent-2411-132: This document, \verb'mnras_guide.tex', provides guidance on using that style file and the features it enables.
elements.sent-2411-133: This is not a general guide on how to use \LaTeX, of which many excellent examples already exist.
elements.sent-2411-134: We particularly recommend Wikibooks \LaTeX\footnote{\url{https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/LaTeX , a collaborative online textbook which is of use to both beginners and experts.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Alternatively there are several other online resources, and most academic libraries also hold suitable beginner's guides.
elements.sent-2411-136: For guidance on the contents of papers, journal style, and how to submit a paper, see the MNRAS Instructions to Authors\footnote{\url{http://www.oxfordjournals.org/our_journals/mnras/for_authors/ .}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Only technical issues with the \LaTeX\ class are considered here.
elements.section-2411-007: Obtaining and installing the MNRAS package
elements.sent-2411-138: Some \LaTeX\ distributions come with the MNRAS package by default.
elements.sent-2411-139: If yours does not, you can either install it using your distribution's package manager, or download it from the Comprehensive \TeX\ Archive Network\footnote{\url{http://www.ctan.org/tex-archive/macros/latex/contrib/mnras (CTAN).}}
elements.sent-2411-140: The files can either be installed permanently by placing them in the appropriate directory (consult the documentation for your \LaTeX\ distribution), or used temporarily by placing them in the working directory for your paper.
elements.sent-2411-141: To use the MNRAS package, simply specify \verb'mnras' as the document class at the start of a \verb'.tex' file:.
elements.sent-2411-142: Then compile \LaTeX\ (and if necessary \bibtex) in the usual way.
elements.sent-2411-143: We recommend that you start with a copy of the mnras\_template.tex file.
elements.sent-2411-144: Rename the file, update the information on the title page, and then work on the text of your paper.
elements.sent-2411-145: Guidelines for content, style etc. are given in the instructions to authors on the journal's website$^{\ref{foot:itas }$.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Note that this document does not follow all the aspects of MNRAS journal style (e.g. it has a table of contents).
elements.sent-2411-147: If a paper is accepted, it is professionally typeset and copyedited by the publishers.
elements.sent-2411-148: It is therefore likely that minor changes to presentation will occur.
elements.sent-2411-149: For this reason, we ask authors to ignore minor details such as slightly long lines, extra blank spaces, or misplaced figures, because these details will be dealt with during the production process.
elements.sent-2411-150: Papers must be submitted electronically via the online submission system; paper submissions are not permitted.
elements.sent-2411-151: For full guidance on how to submit a paper, see the instructions to authors.
elements.sent-2411-152: There are several options which can be added to the document class line like this:.
elements.sent-2411-154: This should be used {\it only if necessary for the display of numerous very long equations.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Please don't submit papers in this format.
elements.sent-2411-156: Only these three are included, if you require other symbols you will need to include the \verb'amsmath' or \verb'amsymb' packages (see section~\ref{sec:packages ).}
elements.sent-2411-157: Some of these options are deprecated and retained for backwards compatibility only.
elements.sent-2411-158: Others are used in almost all papers, but again are retained as options to ensure that papers written decades ago will continue to compile without problems.
elements.sent-2411-159: If you want to include any other packages, see section~\ref{sec:packages .}
elements.sent-2411-160: If you are using mnras\_template.tex the necessary code for generating the title page, headers and footers is already present.
elements.sent-2411-161: Simply edit the title, author list, institutions, abstract and keywords as described below.
elements.sent-2411-162: There are two forms of the title: the full version used on the first page, and a short version which is used in the header of other odd-numbered pages (the `running head').
elements.sent-2411-164: The full title can be multiple lines (use \verb' ' to start a new line) and may be as long as necessary, although we encourage authors to use concise titles.
elements.sent-2411-165: The running head must be $\le~45$ characters on a single line.
elements.sent-2411-166: See appendix~\ref{sec:advanced for more complicated examples.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Like the title, there are two forms of author list: the full version which appears on the title page, and a short form which appears in the header of the even-numbered pages.
elements.sent-2411-169: If the author list is more than one line long, start a new line using \verb'\newauthor'.
elements.sent-2411-170: Use \verb' ' to start the institution list.
elements.sent-2411-171: Affiliations for each author should be indicated with a superscript number, and correspond to the list of institutions below the author list.
elements.sent-2411-172: For example, if I were to write a paper with two coauthors at another institution, one of whom also works at a third location:.
elements.verbatim-2411-004: \author[K. T. Smith et al.]{ Keith T. Smith,$^{1}$ A. N. Other,$^{2}$ and Third Author$^{2,3}$ $^{1}$Affiliation 1 $^{2}$Affiliation 2 $^{3}$Affiliation 3}
elements.sent-2411-173: Affiliations should be in the format `Department, Institution, Street Address, City and Postal Code, Country'.
elements.sent-2411-174: Email addresses can be inserted with the \verb'' command which adds a title page footnote.
elements.sent-2411-175: If you want to list more than one email, put them all in the same \verb'\thanks' and use \verb'\footnotemark[]' to refer to the same footnote multiple times.
elements.sent-2411-176: Present addresses (if different to those where the work was performed) can also be added with a \verb'\thanks' command.
elements.sent-2411-177: The abstract is entered in an \verb'abstract' environment:.
elements.sent-2411-178: Note that there is a word limit on the length of abstracts.
elements.sent-2411-179: For the current word limit, see the journal instructions to authors$^{\ref{foot:itas }$.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Immediately following the abstract, a set of keywords is entered in a \verb'keywords' environment:.
elements.sent-2411-181: There is a list of permitted keywords, which is agreed between all the major astronomy journals and revised every few years.
elements.sent-2411-182: Do not make up new keywords! For the current list of allowed keywords, see the journal's instructions to authors$^{\ref{foot:itas }$.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Sections and lists are generally the same as in the standard \LaTeX\ classes.
elements.sent-2411-184: Sections are entered in the usual way, using \verb'
elements.sent-2411-185: It is possible to nest up to four section levels: \begin{verbatim.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Some sections are not numbered as part of journal style (e.g. the Acknowledgements).
elements.sent-2411-187: To insert an unnumbered section use the `starred' version of the command: \verb'
elements.sent-2411-188: Two forms of lists can be used in MNRAS -- numbered and unnumbered.
elements.sent-2411-189: For a numbered list, use the \verb'enumerate' environment:.
elements.sent-2411-190: Note that the list uses lowercase Roman numerals, rather than the \LaTeX\ default Arabic numerals.
elements.sent-2411-191: For an unnumbered list, use the \verb'description' environment without the optional argument:.
elements.sent-2411-192: Bulleted lists using the \verb'itemize' environment should not be used in MNRAS; it is retained for backwards compatibility only.
elements.sent-2411-193: The MNRAS class mostly adopts standard \LaTeX\ handling of mathematics, which is briefly summarised here.
elements.sent-2411-194: See also section~\ref{sec:packages for packages that support more advanced mathematics.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Mathematics can be inserted into the running text using the syntax \verb'$1+1=2$', which produces $1+1=2$.
elements.sent-2411-196: Use this only for short expressions or when referring to mathematical quantities; equations should be entered as described below.
elements.sent-2411-197: Equations should be entered using the \verb'equation' environment, which automatically numbers them:.
elements.sent-2411-198: If a paper has a large number of equations, it may be better to number them by section (2.1, 2.2 etc.).
elements.sent-2411-199: To do this, add the command \verb'\numberwithin{equation {section ' to the preamble.}}
elements.sent-2411-200: It is also possible to produce un-numbered equations by using the \LaTeX\ built-in \verb'\begin{equation}'\textellipsis\verb'\end{equation}' and \verb'\begin{equation}'\textellipsis\verb'\end{equation}' commands; however MNRAS requires that all equations are numbered, so these commands should be avoided.
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Additional commands for special symbols commonly used in astronomy. These can be used anywhere.} \label{tab:anysymbols} \begin{tabular*}{\columnwidth}{@{}l@{\hspace*{50pt}}l@{\hspace*{50pt}}l@{}} Command Output Meaning \verb'\sun' \sun Sun, solar [2pt] \verb'\earth' \earth Earth, terrestrial [2pt] \verb'\micron' \micron microns [2pt] \verb'\degr' \degr degrees [2pt] \verb'\arcmin' \arcmin arcminutes [2pt] \verb'\arcsec' \arcsec arcseconds [2pt] \verb'\fdg' \fdg fraction of a degree [2pt] \verb'\farcm' \farcm fraction of an arcminute [2pt] \verb'\farcs' \farcs fraction of an arcsecond [2pt] \verb'\fd' \fd fraction of a day [2pt] \verb'\fh' \fh fraction of an hour [2pt] \verb'\fm' \fm fraction of a minute [2pt] \verb'\fs' \fs fraction of a second [2pt] \verb'\fp' \fp fraction of a period [2pt] \verb'\diameter' \diameter diameter [2pt] \verb'\sq' \sq square, Q.E.D. [2pt] \end{tabular*}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Additional commands for mathematical symbols. These can only be used in maths mode.} \label{tab:mathssymbols} \begin{tabular*}{\columnwidth}{l@{\hspace*{40pt}}l@{\hspace*{40pt}}l} Command Output Meaning \verb'\upi' $\upi$ upright pi [2pt] \verb'\umu' $\umu$ upright mu [2pt] \verb'\upartial' $\upartial$ upright partial derivative [2pt] \verb'\lid' $\lid$ less than or equal to [2pt] \verb'\gid' $\gid$ greater than or equal to [2pt] \verb'\la' $\la$ less than of order [2pt] \verb'\ga' $\ga$ greater than of order [2pt] \verb'\loa' $\loa$ less than approximately [2pt] \verb'\goa' $\goa$ greater than approximately [2pt] \verb'\cor' $\cor$ corresponds to [2pt] \verb'\sol' $\sol$ similar to or less than [2pt] \verb'\sog' $\sog$ similar to or greater than [2pt] \verb'\lse' $\lse$ less than or homotopic to [2pt] \verb'\gse' $\gse$ greater than or homotopic to [2pt] \verb'\getsto' $\getsto$ from over to [2pt] \verb'\grole' $\grole$ greater over less [2pt] \verb'\leogr' $\leogr$ less over greater \end{tabular*}
elements.sent-2411-201: Some additional symbols of common use in astronomy have been added in the MNRAS class.
elements.sent-2411-203: The command names are -- as far as possible -- the same as those used in other major astronomy journals.
elements.sent-2411-204: Many other mathematical symbols are also available, either built into \LaTeX\ or via additional packages.
elements.sent-2411-205: If you want to insert a specific symbol but don't know the \LaTeX\ command, we recommend using the Detexify website\footnote{\url{http://detexify.kirelabs.org .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Sometimes font or coding limitations mean a symbol may not get smaller when used in sub- or superscripts, and will therefore be displayed at the wrong size.
elements.sent-2411-207: There is no need to worry about this as it will be corrected by the typesetter during production.
elements.sent-2411-208: To produce bold symbols in mathematics, use \verb'\bmath' for simple variables, and the \verb'bm' package for more complex symbols (see section~\ref{sec:packages ).}
elements.sent-2411-209: Vectors are set in bold italic, using \verb'\mathbfit{ '.}
elements.sent-2411-210: For matrices, use \verb'\mathbfss{ ' to produce a bold sans-serif font e.g. \mathbfss{H ; this works even outside maths mode, but not all symbols are available (e.g. Greek).}}
elements.sent-2411-211: For $\nabla$ (del, used in gradients, divergence etc.) use \verb'$\nabla$'.
elements.sent-2411-212: A new \verb'\ion{ { ' command has been added to the class file, for the correct typesetting of ionisation states.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: For example, to typeset singly ionised calcium use \verb'\ion{Ca {ii ', which produces \ion{Ca {ii .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Figures and tables (collectively called `floats') are mostly the same as built into \LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-215: Figures are inserted in the usual way using a \verb'figure' environment and \verb'\includegraphics'.
elements.sent-2411-216: The example Figure~\ref{fig:example was generated using the code:.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{An example table.} \label{tab:example} \begin{tabular}{lcc} Star Mass Luminosity $M_{\sun}$ $L_{\sun}$ Sun 1.00 1.00 $\alpha$~Cen~A 1.10 1.52 $\epsilon$~Eri 0.82 0.34 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-217: The example Table~\ref{tab:example was generated using the code:.}
elements.verbatim-2411-011: \begin{table} \caption{An example table.} \label{tab:example} \begin{tabular}{lcc} Star Mass Luminosity $M_{\sun}$ $L_{\sun}$ Sun 1.00 1.00 $\alpha$~Cen~A 1.10 1.52 $\epsilon$~Eri 0.82 0.34 \end{tabular} \end{table}
elements.sent-2411-218: Captions go above tables but below figures, as in the examples above.
elements.sent-2411-219: The \LaTeX\ float placement commands \verb'' are intentionally disabled.
elements.sent-2411-220: Layout of figures and tables will be adjusted by the publisher during the production process, so authors should not concern themselves with placement to avoid disappointment and wasted effort.
elements.sent-2411-221: Simply place the \LaTeX\ code close to where the figure or table is first mentioned in the text and leave exact placement to the publishers.
elements.sent-2411-222: By default a figure or table will occupy one column of the page.
elements.sent-2411-223: To produce a wider version which covers both columns, use the \verb'figure*' or \verb'table*' environment.
elements.sent-2411-224: If a figure or table is too long to fit on a single page it can be split it into several parts.
elements.sent-2411-225: Create an additional figure or table which uses \verb'\contcaption{ ' instead of \verb'\caption{ '.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: This will automatically correct the numbering and add `continued' at the start of the caption.
elements.figure-2411-010: \contcaption{A table continued from the previous one.} \label{tab:continued} \begin{tabular}{lcc} Star Mass Luminosity $M_{\sun}$ $L_{\sun}$ $\tau$~Cet 0.78 0.52 $\delta$~Pav 0.99 1.22 $\sigma$~Dra 0.87 0.43 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-227: Table~\ref{tab:continued was generated using the code:.}
elements.verbatim-2411-012: \begin{table} \contcaption{A table continued from the previous one.} \label{tab:continued} \begin{tabular}{lcc} Star Mass Luminosity $M_{\sun}$ $L_{\sun}$ $\tau$~Cet 0.78 0.52 $\delta$~Pav 0.99 1.22 $\sigma$~Dra 0.87 0.43 \end{tabular} \end{table}
elements.sent-2411-228: To produce a landscape figure or table, use the \verb'pdflscape' package and the \verb'landscape' environment.
elements.sent-2411-229: The landscape Table~\ref{tab:landscape was produced using the code:.}
elements.verbatim-2411-013: \begin{landscape} \begin{table} \caption{An example landscape table.} \label{tab:landscape} \begin{tabular}{cccccccccc} Header Header ... Unit Unit ... Data Data ... Data Data ... ... \end{tabular} \end{table} \end{landscape}
elements.sent-2411-230: Unfortunately this method will force a page break before the table appears.
elements.sent-2411-231: More complicated solutions are possible, but authors shouldn't worry about this.. \begin{landscape.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{An example landscape table.} \label{tab:landscape} \begin{tabular}{cccccccccc} Header Header Header Header Header Header Header Header Header Header Unit Unit Unit Unit Unit Unit Unit Unit Unit Unit Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data Data \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-232: The usual \LaTeX\ commands \verb'' and \verb'\ref{ ' can be used for cross-referencing within the same paper.}
elements.sent-2411-233: We recommend that you use these whenever relevant, rather than writing out the section or figure numbers explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-234: This ensures that cross-references are updated whenever the numbering changes (e.g. during revision) and provides clickable links (if available in your compiler).
elements.sent-2411-235: It is best to give each section, figure and table a logical label.
elements.sent-2411-236: For example, Table~\ref{tab:mathssymbols has the label \verb'tab:mathssymbols', whilst section~\ref{sec:packages has the label \verb'sec:packages'.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Add the label after the section or caption command, as in the examples in sections~\ref{sec:sections and \ref{sec:fig_table .}}
elements.sent-2411-238: Enter the cross-reference with a non-breaking space between the type of object and the number, like this: \verb'see Figure~\ref{fig:example '.}
elements.sent-2411-239: The \verb'\autoref{ ' command can be used to automatically fill out the type of object, saving on typing.}
elements.sent-2411-240: It also causes the link to cover the whole phrase rather than just the number, but for that reason is only suitable for single cross-references rather than ranges.
elements.sent-2411-242: MNRAS uses the Harvard -- author (year) -- citation style, e.g. \citet{author2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-243: This is implemented in \LaTeX\ via the \verb'natbib' package, which in turn is included via the \verb'usenatbib' package option (see section~\ref{sec:options ), which should be used in all papers.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Each entry in the reference list has a `key' (see section~\ref{sec:ref_list ) which is used to generate citations.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Citations will include clickable links to the relevant entry in the reference list, if supported by your \LaTeX\ compiler.. \defcitealias{smith2014 {Paper~I.}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \caption{Common citation commands, provided by the natbib package.} \label{tab:natbib} \begin{tabular}{lll} Command Ouput Note \verb'\citet{key}' \citet{smith2014} \verb'\citep{key}' \citep{smith2014} \verb'\citep{key,key2}' \citep{smith2014,jones2015} Multiple papers \verb'\citet[table 4]{key}' \citet[table 4]{smith2014} \verb'\citep[see][figure 7]{key}' \citep[see][figure 7]{smith2014} \verb'\citealt{key}' \citealt{smith2014} For use with manual brackets \verb'\citeauthor{key}' \citeauthor{smith2014} If already cited in close proximity \verb'\defcitealias{key}{Paper~I}' Define an alias (doesn't work in floats) \verb'\citetalias{key}' \citetalias{smith2014} \verb'\citepalias{key}' \citepalias{smith2014} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-247: There are a number of other \verb'natbib' commands which can be used for more complicated citations.
elements.sent-2411-248: The most commonly used ones are listed in Table~\ref{tab:natbib .}
elements.sent-2411-249: For full guidance on their use, consult the \verb'natbib' documentation\footnote{\url{http://www.ctan.org/pkg/natbib .}}
elements.sent-2411-250: If a reference has several authors, \verb'natbib' will automatically use `et al.' if there are more than two authors.
elements.sent-2411-251: However, if a paper has exactly three authors, MNRAS style is to list all three on the first citation and use `et al.' thereafter.
elements.sent-2411-252: If you are using \bibtex\ (see section~\ref{sec:ref_list ) then this is handled automatically.}
elements.sent-2411-253: If not, the \verb'\citet*{ ' and \verb'\citep*{ ' commands can be used at the first citation to include all of the authors.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: An MNRAS \bibtex\ style file, \verb'mnras.bst', is distributed as part of this package.
elements.sent-2411-255: The rest of this section will assume you are using \bibtex.
elements.sent-2411-256: References are entered into a separate \verb'.bib' file in standard \bibtex\ formatting.
elements.sent-2411-257: This can be done manually, or there are several software packages which make editing the \verb'.bib' file much easier.
elements.sent-2411-258: We particularly recommend JabRef\footnote{\url{http://jabref.sourceforge.net/ , which works on all major operating systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Simply copy this into your \verb'.bib' file or into the `BibTeX source' tab in JabRef.
elements.sent-2411-260: Each entry in the \verb'.bib' file must specify a unique `key' to identify the paper, the format of which is up to the author.
elements.sent-2411-261: Simply cite it in the usual way, as described in section~\ref{sec:cite , using the specified key.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Compile the paper as usual, but add an extra step to run the bibtex command.
elements.sent-2411-263: Consult the documentation for your compiler or latex distribution.
elements.sent-2411-264: Correct formatting of the reference list will be handled by \bibtex\ in almost all cases, provided that the correct information was entered into the \verb'.bib' file.
elements.sent-2411-265: Note that ADS entries are not always correct, particularly for older papers and conference proceedings, so may need to be edited.
elements.sent-2411-266: If in doubt, or if you are producing the reference list manually, see the MNRAS instructions to authors$^{\ref{foot:itas }$ for the current guidelines on how to format the list of references.}
elements.sent-2411-267: To start an appendix, simply place the \verb' ' command before the next \verb'
elements.sent-2411-268: This will automatically adjust the section headings, figures, tables, and equations to reflect the fact that they are part of an appendix.
elements.sent-2411-269: It is only necessary to enter the \verb' ' command once -- everything after that command is in an appendix.
elements.sent-2411-270: Remember that appendices should be placed after the list of references.
elements.sent-2411-271: Unlike other astronomy class files, there are no special commands for online material.
elements.sent-2411-272: If your paper has any online material, it should be placed in a separate file.
elements.sent-2411-273: See our instructions to authors$^{\ref{foot:itas }$ for guidance.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Sometimes authors need to include additional \LaTeX\ packages, which provide extra features.
elements.sent-2411-275: For example, the \verb'bm' package provides extra bold maths symbols, whilst the \verb'pdflscape' package adds support for landscape pages.
elements.sent-2411-276: Packages can be included by adding the \verb'\usepackage{ ' command to the preamble of the document (not the main body).}
elements.sent-2411-277: Please only include packages which are actually used in the paper, and include a comment to explain what each one does.
elements.sent-2411-279: If you are using mnras\_template.tex, it includes a specific section for this purpose, near the start of the file with the header 'authors - place your own packages here'.
elements.sent-2411-281: Consult the documentation for that package for instructions on how to use the additional features.
elements.sent-2411-282: Authors should avoid duplicating or redefining commands which are already available in \LaTeX\ or \verb'mnras.cls'.
elements.sent-2411-283: However it may sometimes be necessary to introduce a custom command e.g. as a shortcut while writing the paper.
elements.sent-2411-284: Please only include commands which are actually used in the paper, and include a comment to explain what each one does.
elements.sent-2411-285: Use \verb'\newcommand', not \verb'\def', as this will avoid accidentally overwriting existing commands.
elements.sent-2411-286: Place custom commands in the preamble of the document (not the main body).
elements.sent-2411-287: If you are using mnras\_template.tex, it includes a specific section for this purpose, near the start of the file with the header 'authors - place your own commands here'.
elements.sent-2411-288: As an example, a shortcut for the unit \kms can be defined like this:.
elements.sent-2411-289: Velocities can then be written as e.g. \verb'2.3\kms' which produces 2.3\kms.
elements.sent-2411-290: Similar shortcuts can be used for frequently quoted object designations.
elements.sent-2411-291: Some code segments are reproduced from, and some examples are based upon, that guide.
elements.sent-2411-292: The authors were: A.~Woollatt, M.~Reed, R.~Mulvey, K.~Matthews, D.~Starling, Y.~Yu, A.~Richardson (all CUP), and Penny~Smith, N.~Thompson and Gregor~Hutton (all Blackwell), whose work is gratefully acknowledged.
elements.sent-2411-293: The accompanying \bibtex\ style file was written by John Sleath, Tim Jenness and Norman Gray, without whom \bibtex\ support would not have been possible.
elements.sent-2411-294: Some special symbols in tables~\ref{tab:anysymbols --\ref{tab:mathssymbols were taken from the Springer Verlag Astronomy \& Astrophysics \LaTeX\ class, with their permission.}}
elements.sent-2411-295: KTS thanks Nelson Beebe (University of Utah) for helpful advice regarding CTAN.
elements.sent-2411-296: The inclusion of a Data Availability Statement is a requirement for articles published in MNRAS.
elements.sent-2411-297: Data Availability Statements provide a standardised format for readers to understand the availability of data underlying the research results described in the article.
elements.sent-2411-298: The statement may refer to original data generated in the course of the study or to third-party data analysed in the article.
elements.sent-2411-299: The statement should describe and provide means of access, where possible, by linking to the data or providing the required accession numbers for the relevant databases or DOIs.. .
elements.sent-2411-300: Abbreviations for cited journals can be accessed using the commands listed in table~\ref{tab:journal_abbr .}
elements.sent-2411-301: Although some of these may appear to be outdated or rarely cited, they have been selected to be compatible with the \bibtex\ output by the NASA Astrophysics Data System$^{\ref{foot:ads }$, commands used by other astronomy journals, and with additional entries for journals with non-standard abbreviations in MNRAS.}
elements.sent-2411-302: For journals which are not on this list, see our instructions to authors$^{\ref{foot:itas }$ for guidance on how to abbreviate titles.}

elements.sent-2411-303: Sometimes formatting doesn't behave exactly as expected when used in titles or section headings, and must be modified to obtain the correct appearance.
elements.sent-2411-304: Generally the publishers can fix these problems during the typesetting process after a paper is accepted, but authors may wish to adjust these themselves to minimise the possibility of errors and/or for the benefit of the refereeing process.
elements.sent-2411-305: Below are some examples of output, followed by the \LaTeX\ code which produces them.
elements.sent-2411-306: Most mathematics and text formatting works as expected, but some commands might not be the correct size, bold or italic.
elements.sent-2411-307: If so they can be finessed by hand, as in the bold mathematics here: \boxit{ \bf Herschel observations of galaxies at $\bm{\delta > 60\degr}$}.
elements.sent-2411-308: Most fonts do not provide bold and italic versions of small capitals, so the \verb'\ion{ { ' command doesn't produce the expected output in headings.}}
elements.sent-2411-309: The effect has to be `faked' using font size commands, remembering that the running head is a different style: \boxit{ \bf Abundances in H\,{ II regions.}}
elements.verbatim-2411-017: \title [Abundances in H\,{ II} regions] {Abundances in H\,{ II} regions}
elements.sent-2411-310: Complex mathematics can cause problems with links, so might require adding a less formatted short version of the heading: \boxit{\bf 2\quad FINDING Mg\,{\sevensize II ABSORBERS AT $\bm{z > 2}$}.}
elements.verbatim-2411-018: \section [Finding Mg II absorbers at z > 2] {Finding M\lowercase{g}\,{\sevensize II} absorbers at $\lowercase{\bm{z > 2}}$}
elements.sent-2411-311: Using square brackets in headings can cause additional linking problems, which are solved by wrapping them in \{\textellipsis\}: \boxit{\bf 2.1\quad [C\,{\sevensize II ] 158$\bmath{\umu}$m emission.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: Use \verb'\text{ ' (not \verb'\rm') for non-variables in mathematics, which preserves the formatting of the surrounding text.}
elements.sent-2411-313: For the same reasons, use \verb'' for italics (not \verb'\it').
elements.sent-2411-314: The following commands are available for the use of editors and production staff only.
elements.sent-2411-315: They should not be used (or modified in the template) by authors.
elements.sent-2411-316: The standard template generates this automatically, starting from 1.
elements.sent-2411-317: The command name refers to Blackwell Science Publishing, who were the publishers at the time when MNRAS began accepting \LaTeX\ submissions in 1993.
elements.sent-2411-318: It should not be used manually.
